citations.narrative:
The licensee posts live videos on their online exhibition account (TikTok). In this video recording dated 2/24/2026, it
displays the licensee's husband changing the diaper for a capuchin monkey. In the video, the person is grabbing the
animal by its tail, holding the animal upside down by it's tail, and handling it roughly while the capuchin seems to be in
distress. At the time of inspection, the licensee acknowledged that this is her husband in the video and their animal. Video
was recorded after they received their license.
In a video recording dated 3/24/2026, two spider monkeys are shown to be consuming dipping tobacco off the leg of an
individual. It can be heard in the video, that the person is encouraging them to consume the dipping tobacco. Dipping
tobacco poses a potential risk to the animals. They could be harmed by ingesting or coming into contact with its toxic
components. At the time of inspection, the licensee acknowledged that this is her husband in the video and their animals.
Video was recorded after they received their license.
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma,
behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.
CORRECTION DATE: 08 MAY 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Alexandra Calderon, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.131(b)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
The licensee posts live videos on their online exhibition account (TikTok). In this video recording dated 2/24/2026, it 
displays the licensee's husband changing the diaper for a capuchin monkey. In the video, the person is grabbing the 
animal by its tail, holding the animal upside down by it's tail, and handling it roughly while the capuchin seems to be in 
distress. At the time of inspection, the licensee acknowledged that this is her husband in the video and their animal. Video 
was recorded after they received their license.  
 
In a video recording dated 3/24/2026, two spider monkeys are shown to be consuming dipping tobacco off the leg of an 
individual. It can be heard in the video, that the person is encouraging them to consume the dipping tobacco. Dipping 
tobacco poses a potential risk to the animals. They could be harmed by ingesting or coming into contact with its toxic 
components. At the time of inspection, the licensee acknowledged that this is her husband in the video and their animals. 
Video was recorded after they received their license.  
 
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, 
behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.  
 
 
CORRECTION DATE: 08 MAY 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Alexandra Calderon, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
